title: AI-use memo
text:
This memo documents how I used generative AI to complete the case study.
My approach was to using AI was supervisory and efficient: I used AI to execute coding, planning, data-processing, and repetitive work (e.g.,implementation of Tasks 1–3, generate tables/figures, build the website, write documentation) so I could dedicate more time on the analytic and judgment-heavy parts — the Task 4 scoping and ranking, the design logic in Task 3, and the interpretation of results. I specifically chose not to use AI in a first pass in Task 4 so you can see my own thinking process. Wherever possible, I ran multiple agents in parallel (e.g., letting an adversarial tester stress-test Task 1 and 2 while I worked on Task 4), and I used AI heavily to copy edit, streamline, and pressure-test what I had already written. Nothing went in unreviewed — I read all output, verified the numbers independently, and edited for improvements.


A few comments on how I used AI to tie the whole approach together:

Division of labor. I pushed the repetitive and mechanical work (implementing functions, generating 48 crosstab tables and the figures, wiring the website) to AI, and reserved my own time for the judgment calls — the Task 4 scoping and ranking, the Task 3 design, and every interpretation. On the judgment tasks I wrote first and used AI to evaluate my reasoning, so the logic on display is mine.
Parallelism for throughput. Because agents run in the background, I could launch a stress test or an editorial review and keep working on a different task in the meantime — closer to running a small team than to a single chat.
AI in specialized roles. Rather than one general assistant, I set up distinct agents for building, adversarially testing, editing, and committing — each with a narrow, well-defined job.
A verification-first loop. For every non-trivial piece: plan → build → independently break/verify → fix → re-verify, with the numbers checked against a second method before I accepted anything.
Automating the documentation itself. Tracking models, prompts, and corrections by hand as I worked would have been tedious and error-prone, and a drain on time better spent on the analysis. So I had the harness do it: the prompt-capture hook logged every prompt verbatim, a running ai_log.md recorded decisions and corrections in real time, and this memo was then drafted from that raw material for me to edit. Meeting the assignment’s documentation requirement cost me almost none of my analytic time — I orchestrated it rather than transcribed it.
Fittingly, Task 3 asks me to design an AI-assisted workflow — and the harnessing I built for this submission (tiered agents, a human always on the costly call, automatic logging) is a small live demonstration of the same instincts.





Claude Opus 4.8, via Claude Code, for the coding, data processing, drafting, and harnessing. I chose it because it’s the strongest available model for code and reasoning, and the 1M context let me keep the whole project in view at once (data dictionary, many source files, a long thread) instead of re-establishing state each time.
The Task 3 design proposes a different model strategy for the system it describes — cheap Claude Haiku for first-pass triage, escalating uncertain cases to Sonnet/Opus. That’s a design recommendation for a production pipeline, not what I used to build this submission.




Not a browser chat window — I used Claude Code, an agentic CLI/IDE harness inside Positron (a VSCode fork developed by Posit for R and Python users), so the model could read/write files, run R (Rscript), render the Quarto site, and manage git directly while I reviewed and directed each step. That let me work at the level of “here’s the design — implement it, then stop for my verification” rather than copy-pasting code back and forth. Because the panel data is already anonymized (respondent IDs are opaque tokens, not names or contact details), processing it locally through the model raised no confidentiality concern.



I used a few skills and agents to harness Claude’s power and make the work steerable, checkable, and parallelizable:

A project CLAUDE.md for persistent context. Before writing any analysis, I had the AI run a QA/profiling pass over all four data files and used it to build a verified data dictionary (columns, types, keys, join cardinality, weight distribution) and a list of data wrinkles to watch for (the ~23% missing-ID rows, the duplicate rows, the ambiguous “last 90 days” reference date, demographic NAs). I captured that — together with standing rules (use the survey package; portable base-R/tidyverse; house style) — in a CLAUDE.md steering file the model automatically reads on every prompt. So the AI started each task already knowing the data and my conventions, and I never had to re-establish that context.
Three specialized subagents, so AI played distinct roles instead of one monolithic chat:

adversarial-tester — a read-only agent whose job is to break my code with synthetic known-answer tests and edge cases, not praise it.
git — prepares and stages commits to a consistent convention and stops for my approval.
content-editor — reviews my own Task 3/4 writing for typos, clarity, prompt-coverage, and gaps, while explicitly preserving my voice rather than rewriting into generic prose.

A UserPromptSubmit hook (added via the update-config skill) that auto-captures every prompt I submit, verbatim, to notes/prompt-capture.log — model-independent, so it can’t drift the way a model-maintained log does. It’s the source for section 4.
A reusable utils/ toolbox (read_survey(), weighted_crosstab(), density.R), each roxygen-documented so it could drop into a Verasight analysis package rather than live as one-off scripts.
A Quarto website to present the four write-ups, which I had the AI build quickly by modeling it on the structure of another repo I maintain — so the navigation, layout, and build config came together in minutes rather than from scratch.




These are pulled verbatim from notes/prompt-capture.log (the hook’s record of every prompt I submitted); the longer ones are excerpted where marked. They show my usual pattern — specify the design and the verification plan up front, then hand implementation to the model — across a few modes of use:
(a) Directing a build, with the verification plan stated up front (Task 1):
Task 1 of the Verasight case study. Before writing anything, read CLAUDE.md (data dictionary, conventions, known data wrinkles), notes/style.md (table styling), and utils/read_survey.R (use it to load data).

GOAL
Write a single, well-documented, reusable R function (plus small internal helpers if needed) named weighted_crosstab(), and store the FUNCTION DEFINITION in utils/weighted_crosstab.R (filename matches the function; part of the shared toolbox). Then EXECUTE it in code/01_task1_crosstabs.R, which sources the function (source("utils/weighted_crosstab.R")) and runs it on the recent survey to generate the tables. Keep definition (utils/) and execution (code/) separate.

The function produces weighted proportion tables — one for each survey outcome × demographic variable — matching the attached example, with each question's full wording shown just above its table.

SEMANTICS (match the example exactly)
- Rows = outcome response levels; columns = demographic categories.
- Cells = COLUMN proportions: within each demographic category, the response levels sum to 100%. Round to whole percentages. Preserve the outcome's factor-level order for rows (so e.g. a "Don't Know" level still appears even at 0%).

WEIGHTING
- These are complex-survey weights (verified: all non-integer, most < 1, sum ≈ n) — NOT frequency weights. Compute weighted proportions with the `survey` package. No strata/PSU were provided, so build the design as svydesign(ids = ~1, weights = ~<weight_col>, data = ...) and use svyby/svymean. Do not use unweighted counts.

QUESTION WORDING
- Attach each outcome's full question text as a title/subtitle above its table, looked up from the reference file (join variable name -> label). Take the reference as an argument.

REUSABILITY (this is the grading bar — a teammate must run it on a DIFFERENT Verasight survey without editing internals)
- Infer variable roles BY DEFAULT: outcomes = columns matching `^q[0-9]+$`; demographics = all remaining columns except the id and weight columns. Exclude `respondent_id` and `weight` from analysis — they aid analysis, they aren't outcomes.
- But EXPOSE OVERRIDE ARGUMENTS so nothing is hardcoded: outcomes, demographics, id_col = "respondent_id", weight_col = "weight", reference, and the outcome-detection pattern. Explicit args win over inference.

ROBUSTNESS
- Handle NA demographic categories and NA responses explicitly (document the choice — e.g. drop NA demographic groups but keep responses visible — rather than silently dropping).
- Fail with informative errors on: missing weight column, an outcome/demographic not in the data, an empty subgroup, or a non-factor outcome that can't be tabulated.

OUTPUT
- Style per notes/style.md: clean gt table — bold row labels, right-aligned %, subtle row shading, a vertical rule after the "Response" column. Also RETURN the tables as a named list (keyed by outcome × demographic) so they're programmatically accessible, not just printed.

DOCS & PORTABILITY
- roxygen-style header on the function: one-line purpose, @param for every argument, @return, a runnable @examples block. Comment any non-obvious logic.
- Keep deps to survey + tidyverse + gt. Don't over-engineer — one primary function.

code/01_task1_crosstabs.R should: source utils/read_survey.R and utils/weighted_crosstab.R, load data/2024-054_responses.rds and its reference, call weighted_crosstab(), and render the tables for the full set of outcome × demographic pairs (or a clear representative subset).

STOP after writing both files. I'll run them through the adversarial-tester agent and do an independent base-R weighted.mean() hand-check before we accept.
(b) Framing the analysis and naming the assumptions myself (Task 2, full):
Task 2: I have a survey response database in data/response-db.rds. Each row is a survey
response with a user ID and dates (inspect the structure first).

I need to analyze "response density" — how concentrated survey responses
are among panelists. Three tasks:

1. Find the smallest percent of users who account for 50% of all responses.
2. Same metric, restricted to users who registered in the last 90 days
   (use the latest date in the data as the reference point, and state that 
   assumption).
3. Visualize the full concentration curve: cumulative % of responses vs.
   cumulative % of users (most active first), with the 10%–90% thresholds
   marked. Save the output to output/task2/ as .html file since we'll pull this 
   into our write up later.

Use R with tidyverse. Write clean, commented code, report the numeric
answers, and briefly summarize the results — including any assumptions
or edge cases (ties, users with zero responses, etc.). Name the file you create code/02_task2_density.R
(c) A short corrective steer, catching a data-handling problem (full):
I'm confused on how this function is handling NAs or NaNs? Are they only on survey ids? They should be dropped and should not enter the analysis -- I don't want any silent failing here.  
(d) Using AI to critique my own work rather than write it (Task 4):
Use the content editor agent on task4.qmd while I work on task3.qmd
Here I’d written the Task 4 answers myself and used the content-editor agent to pressure-test them for gaps and consistency while I worked on Task 3 — AI reviewing my reasoning, not producing it.



I treated verification as a first-class step. Several of these checks are agents reviewing AI-written code — which carries a correlated-error risk — so I leaned hardest on the checks that don’t share it: independent code paths, known-answer tests, and my own hand-tracing. Specifically, I traced the density metric by hand on a small example, confirmed the synthetic cases were correct tests before trusting them, and spot-checked raw records against the computed figures.

Independent recomputation. For Task 1 I recomputed a table cell three ways — the function, base-R weighted.mean(), and survey::svymean() on a separate design — and confirmed they matched to floating-point precision (shown live in the Task 1 write-up).
Adversarial testing with known answers. The adversarial-tester agent checked the density metric against synthetic cases with known results (e.g., 1 of 10 users holding 50% of responses → 10%) and reproduced the headline numbers via a fully independent code path. These weren’t rubber stamps — the synthetic cases surfaced a real bug (negative weights would silently produce negative percentages), which I fixed and re-ran to confirm.
A data-profiling pass before any analysis, which caught several data-quality issues (detailed below).
Verifying the specification, not just the arithmetic. I checked the analytic choices as much as the math — that “last 90 days” meant 90 days back from the latest date in the data (not today), how ties and zero-response users were handled in the concentration metric, and which user universe the denominator used — and stated each assumption explicitly, since a correct computation of the wrong quantity is still wrong.
Running and rendering everything — every script executes and every report renders; I read the actual outputs rather than trusting that code “looks right.”
AI as a critic of my own writing — the content-editor agent for a final accuracy, consistency, and coverage pass over Tasks 3 and 4.




Some representative examples of where I overrode or redirected the AI:

Task 1 — continuous age swept in as a demographic. The literal inference rule I described in planning would have treated the continuous age column as a ~70-category demographic; I had it refined to categorical-only inference plus a guard that errors on continuous variables.
Task 1 — a robustness fix I directed. After the tester surfaced the negative-weight bug (§5), I had a non-negativity guard added so the function errors on invalid weights instead of silently returning nonsense — the kind of edge case a teammate reusing it would eventually hit.
Task 2 — the “22% mega-user” that wasn’t. The model’s first pass counted responses with a plain count(), which lumped all 43,692 missing-ID rows into a single NA “user” holding 22% of responses. I caught it in the profiling output and directed that unattributable rows be dropped — moving the headline answer from a misleading ~9.9% to the correct 15.67%.
Task 4 — a factual slip I wrote, caught on review. The content-editor flagged that my ranking assumed the longitudinal waves spanned “6 months to a year,” contradicting the prompt’s “each a month apart”; I corrected it to ~3–4 months.
Disagreeing with the AI critic. The tester also proposed changing a boundary check to >=; I declined it because it would have made the inference and guard logic inconsistent.
Tooling steers. I steered the estimation engine to the survey package (not plain weighted means), and when a version mismatch broke ggplotly(), I had the package updated rather than accept the silent fallback.
This memo. I’m editing the AI’s draft of this document for accuracy and voice — itself an instance of not shipping AI output unchecked.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text: Task 3. Design a fully or partially automated workflow to detect potentially fraudulent open-ended survey responses (e.g., “What is the most important issue facing the US?”) across many surveys — what tools/methods at each stage, why this structure, how to validate it, and how to iterate. (Prompt paraphrased from the 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 3.)

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Summary
You’ve got huge volumes of open-ended answers across many surveys, and some are fake (bots, survey farms, people speed-clicking, AI-written). Reading them all by hand is impossible, so I’d build a three-tier funnel:

Tier 1 (cheap filters): runs cheap automatic checks on every response and gives each a risk score — the obviously-fine majority passes straight through.
Tier 2 (LLM): only the flagged minority goes to an LLM, which reads each one against a fixed rubric and labels it fraud / genuine / uncertain with a confidence.
Tier 3 (humans): people make the costly calls (actual removals) and spot-check both the flagged and the cleared piles to catch mistakes; their decisions become labels that make Tier 1 and the rubric smarter over time.

Throughout: never remove a real respondent on one weak signal — make independent signals (text + closed-ended + paradata) agree and get a human sign-off; keep it cheap by only sending the hard cases to the LLM; keep every step auditable in case we have to defend a removal to a client or panelist; and keep improving it as fraudsters adapt.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Design principles

Defense in depth. Different fraud types (bots, farms, inattentive humans, AI-assisted) fail different checks. A fraudulent response must beat all layers, not one.
Cheap first. Deterministic filters are fast, explainable, and cheap; reserve the LLM for genuine judgment calls on cases we’ve already flagged.
Corroboration before removal. A single weak signal should not remove a respondent because it could be wrong; instead, independent signals should agree.
Asymmetric cost. Optimize for high precision on the “remove” decision (false positives hurt real people and the panel); recover recall via the LLM + human tiers, not by lowering the auto-removal bar.
Human-in-the-loop + auditable. Keep a person on the final costly call; log why each response was flagged and if/why ultimately removed.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Architecture




flowchart TD
  A[Open-ended responses<br/>+ paradata + closed-ended] --> B[Tier 1: cheap signals<br/>dupes, length, timing, gibberish, off-topic]
  B -->|risk score| C{Flagged?}
  C -->|No, low risk| K[Auto-accept<br/>+ random audit sample]
  C -->|Yes| D[Tier 2: LLM classifier<br/>rubric + structured output]
  D -->|confident genuine| K
  D -->|confident fraud| E[Tier 3: human review<br/>adjudicate removals]
  D -->|uncertain / abstain| E
  E --> F[(Gold labels)]
  F -.retrain weights, tune thresholds, update rubric.-> B
  F -.-> D

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Tier 1: Cheap, deterministic signals
Every response gets a vector of signals and a combined risk score; the score orders a review queue and decides what escalates. Start with transparent rules + a logistic model over the signals (interpretable, easy to defend), not a black box.







Signal
What it catches




Exact + near-duplicate (within and across respondents)
Copy-paste, templated bot/farm answers, boilerplate


Length / low-effort
One-word, empty-ish, or filler responses


Gibberish / keyboard-mash
Non-language, random characters


Off-topic / non-responsive
Answers that don’t address the question


Copy-paste / web-lifted
Verbatim from the prompt or the web


AI-generated markers (weak, see Risks)
LLM-assisted text


Paradata + closed-ended (see below)
Speeding, straightlining, bad device/IP



Output: a flags table keyed by response_id with each signal, the score, and the reason codes — so a reviewer (or a client) can see why something surfaced.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
section: Tier 2: LLM classification on the flagged subset only
text:
Tier 2: LLM classification on the flagged subset only
Only responses above the risk threshold go to the LLM, with the question text, the response, and the Tier-1 signals as context (so the model corroborates rather than starts blind).

Model tiering to control cost: a fast cheap model (Claude Haiku) triages; escalate only low-confidence or high-stakes cases to a stronger model (Claude Sonnet/Opus).
Fixed rubric + structured output. The model returns JSON: a category, an is_fraud flag, a calibrated confidence, a short rationale, and which signals it relied on. An explicit uncertain / abstain option routes to humans instead of guessing.
Stack: the Anthropic SDK (Python/R); prompt-cache the rubric across the batch; log prompts + outputs by version.
Guardrails: the model judges authenticity and effort, never the respondent’s opinion, dialect, or brevity-if-on-topic. Short, unpopular, or plainly written answers are not fraud.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Tier 3: Human review (two loops)

Adjudicate the costly calls. Every auto-removal (or a high sample of them) gets a human sign-off before a respondent is dropped.
Audit both classes. Sample the LLM’s cleared responses too — not just the flagged ones — to catch false negatives, plus all low-confidence cases. Stratify: all removals, a random sample of “genuine”, and the uncertain middle.

Reviewers’ decisions become the gold-label set and feed back into Tier-1 weights, thresholds, and the rubric. Track inter-rater reliability (Cohen’s/Fleiss’ κ) so the labels themselves are trustworthy.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
section: Why tier instead of sending everything to an LLM
text:
Why tier instead of sending everything to an LLM

Cost & scale. Across many surveys that’s millions of open-ends; an LLM on each is expensive and slow. Cheap signals dispatch the easy 90–95% for near-nothing.
Precision & explainability. Exact-duplicate and gibberish checks are deterministic and defensible; an LLM alone gives a probabilistic verdict that’s harder to justify to a client.
Robustness. One model is a single point of failure — it can be gamed, drifts, and isn’t calibrated to a survey’s fraud base rate. Independent layers are harder to beat.
Focus. The LLM spends its budget where judgment actually helps (subtle off-topic, AI-assisted, borderline effort), not on obvious junk or obvious genuine answers.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Corroboration: closed-ended answers + paradata
Text signals get much stronger when independent, hard-to-fake metadata agrees — this is the core of “don’t rely on one mechanism”:

Closed-ended: straightlining/pattern responding on grids, failed attention/trap items, logically impossible or contradictory answers (incl. across waves), rare improbable combinations.
Paradata: total duration and per-item latency (speeding), keystroke/paste events, device & browser fingerprint reuse, IP reputation (datacenter/VPN/proxy), geolocation vs. claimed region, multiple completes from one fingerprint.

Rule of thumb: a gibberish open-end and speeding and a datacenter IP and straightlining is a confident removal; any one of those alone is only a flag for the next tier.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Validation

Build a labeled gold set across surveys and question types — human-adjudicated, stratified to include known fraud (past removals, seeded/honeypot responses, attention-check failures) and confirmed genuine.
Metrics with the cost asymmetry front and center. Report precision/recall/F1 per category, but choose the operating point on a cost-weighted basis: a false positive (dropping a real respondent) is weighted far more heavily than a false negative that the human tier can still catch. Show the confusion matrix and a precision–recall curve; pick the threshold, don’t inherit it.
Calibrate the LLM’s confidence (reliability diagram); if “0.9” isn’t right ~90% of the time, recalibrate before trusting it for routing.
Estimate recall you can’t see directly by seeding known-fraud responses and measuring catch rate.
Monitor per survey / per question, since base rates and fraud tactics vary; watch reviewer-overturn rate as a live quality signal.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Iterating to improve accuracy

Closed feedback loop: every human label retrains Tier-1 weights and re-tunes thresholds; recurring misses become new deterministic signals.
Active learning: prioritize labeling the most informative cases (near the threshold, or where LLM and signals disagree) to spend review time well.
Drift watch: fraud evolves — especially AI-generated text. Track signal distributions, LLM–signal agreement, and overturn rate over time; alarm on shifts.
Version & shadow-test: version rubric/prompts/thresholds; run a new version in shadow mode (scores but doesn’t act) against the current one before it can remove anyone.
Error analysis cadence: a standing review of false pos/neg to feed the next iteration.

title: Task 3: Detecting Fraudulent Open-Ended Responses
text:
Risks & limitations

AI-text detection is unreliable. Perplexity/“AI detector” signals have high false-positive rates and penalize non-native and plain writing. Keep them as soft inputs, never a sole basis for removal.
False-positive harm is real: dropping genuine respondents damages data quality, panel trust, and retention — hence corroboration + human sign-off on every removal.
Adversaries adapt; treat this as an evolving system, not a one-time filter — the iteration loop is the point.

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
text: Task 1 — Construct and run a tabulation function. Create a single, documented, reusable R function (plus any small helpers it needs) to produce weighted proportion tables for each survey outcome by each demographic variable in the recent response dataset (2024-054_responses.rds), with the question wording attached. These data are from a nationally representative survey of U.S. adults. Your function should be constructed such that a teammate could understand and use your function on a different Verasight survey without editing its internals.

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
text:
What I built
A single reusable function, weighted_crosstab() (defined in utils/weighted_crosstab.R), that produces weighted proportion tables — one for each survey outcome × demographic — with each question’s wording attached above its table. The execution script code/01_task1_crosstabs.R runs it on survey 2024-054.
The design goal was the grading bar: a teammate can run it on a different Verasight survey without editing any internals. Everything survey-specific is an argument, not hardcoded.

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
text:
How it works
At a high level:

Weighted the right way. These are complex-survey weights (not frequency weights), so estimation uses the survey package (svydesign → svytable), normalized so each demographic column sums to 100% across the response levels.
Question wording is joined from the reference file and shown as each table’s title.
Reusable by design. Roles are inferred by default (outcomes match ^q[0-9]+$; demographics are the remaining categorical columns), but every role is an argument you can override — so the same function runs on a different survey without touching its internals.
Safe by default. It handles missing values, keeps empty response levels at 0%, hard-skips continuous columns like raw age, and errors on negative weights.

The function carries full roxygen documentation (@param / @return / @examples), so it could drop straight into a Verasight analysis package as a documented, reusable helper rather than living as a one-off script.

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
text:
Example: survey 2024-054


Code
responses <- read_survey(here::here("data", "2024-054_responses.rds"), quiet = TRUE)
reference <- read_survey(here::here("data", "2024-054_reference.rds"), quiet = TRUE)

tables <- weighted_crosstab(responses, reference = reference)

# 8 outcomes (q28–q35) × 6 demographics = 48 tables. Show the accuracy-by-age table:
tables[["q28__age_group4"]]$gt






  
    
      In general, how accurate do you think public opinion surveys are?
    
    
  
  
    
      Response
      
        age_group4
      
    
    
      18-29
      30-49
      50-64
      65+
    
  
  
    Extremely accurate
11%
11%
4%
2%
    Very accurate
29%
34%
23%
22%
    Somewhat accurate
54%
48%
65%
65%
    Not very accurate
7%
6%
8%
11%
    Not at all accurate
0%
1%
1%
1%

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
text:
Manual validation
To confirm the numbers are right — and that the survey package (not an unweighted count) is doing the work — I independently recompute one cell of the table above by two other paths and compare:

tab     <- tables[["q28__age_group4"]]$data
cell_fn <- tab[tab$Response == "Extremely accurate", "18-29", drop = TRUE]     # function output

# Path A — base R weighted.mean() (no survey package at all)
sub        <- responses[!is.na(responses$age_group4) &
                          responses$age_group4 == "18-29" & !is.na(responses$q28), ]
cell_wmean <- weighted.mean(sub$q28 == "Extremely accurate", sub$weight)

# Path B — survey::svymean() on an independent design (a different survey-package call)
d        <- svydesign(ids = ~1, weights = ~weight, data = sub)
cell_svy <- as.numeric(svymean(~ I(q28 == "Extremely accurate"), d)[2])

data.frame(
  method   = c("weighted_crosstab()", "base R weighted.mean()", "survey::svymean()"),
  estimate = round(c(cell_fn, cell_wmean, cell_svy), 7)
)

                  method  estimate
1    weighted_crosstab() 0.1098758
2 base R weighted.mean() 0.1098758
3      survey::svymean() 0.1098758



stopifnot(max(abs(c(cell_fn - cell_wmean, cell_fn - cell_svy))) < 1e-9)   # hard check
"All three methods agree to within 1e-9."

[1] "All three methods agree to within 1e-9."


Inside weighted_crosstab() the estimation is done by survey::svydesign() + survey::svytable(), so this agreement confirms the weights are applied through the survey design — not a plain frequency count.

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
section: It works on a different colleague’s survey
text:
It works on a different colleague’s survey
To show portability, here is a synthetic survey a colleague might bring — completely different column names (user_id, wt, item_1/item_2, region2, agegrp) and a differently-named reference. The function is driven entirely by arguments; nothing inside it changes.


Code
set.seed(42)
n <- 400
colleague <- tibble(
  user_id = sprintf("U%04d", seq_len(n)),
  wt      = round(runif(n, 0.5, 2.5), 3),                                   # different weight name
  item_1  = factor(sample(c("Agree", "Neutral", "Disagree"), n, TRUE, c(.5, .3, .2)),
                   levels = c("Agree", "Neutral", "Disagree")),
  item_2  = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), n, TRUE), levels = c("Yes", "No")),
  region2 = factor(sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), n, TRUE)),
  agegrp  = factor(sample(c("<30", "30-59", "60+"), n, TRUE), levels = c("<30", "30-59", "60+"))
)
colleague_ref <- tibble(
  qvar  = c("item_1", "item_2"),
  qtext = c("Our product is easy to use.", "Would you recommend us?")
)

ct <- weighted_crosstab(
  colleague,
  weight_col      = "wt",
  id_col          = "user_id",
  outcome_pattern = "^item_[0-9]+$",     # different question naming scheme
  reference       = colleague_ref,
  ref_var         = "qvar",
  ref_label       = "qtext"
)

names(ct)                                 # inferred item_1/item_2 × region2/agegrp


[1] "item_1__region2" "item_1__agegrp"  "item_2__region2" "item_2__agegrp" 


Code
ct[["item_1__region2"]]$gt                # wording joined from the renamed reference






  
    
      Our product is easy to use.
    
    
  
  
    
      Response
      
        region2
      
    
    
      East
      North
      South
      West
    
  
  
    Agree
48%
54%
55%
55%
    Neutral
32%
30%
23%
28%
    Disagree
21%
17%
22%
17%
  
  
  




The same function, unmodified, tabulated a survey with new names and joined wording from a renamed reference — confirming the reusability requirement.

title: Task 1: Weighted Crosstab Function
text:
Verification

Manual validation (above) recomputes a table cell by two independent paths — base-R weighted.mean() and survey::svymean() — and both match weighted_crosstab() exactly.
Adversarial testing. A dedicated agent stress-tested the function: the colleague-rename simulation above, plus edge cases (missing/misnamed weight, NA demographics and responses, single-level factors, empty subgroups, continuous demographics). It confirmed column proportions sum to 100% and surfaced one defect — negative weights silently yielding negative percentages — which was fixed by adding a non-negativity check.

title: Verasight Data Scientist Case Study
text:
This site presents my submission for the Verasight Data Scientist case study. The analyses are written in R (with a small reusable helper toolbox in utils/), and each task has its own page — use the Tasks menu above, or the links below.
The case study provided four data files: a sample of registered panelists, one recent survey’s responses, a question-reference file, and an aggregate response database spanning many 2024 Verasight projects.

title: Verasight Data Scientist Case Study
text:
The four tasks

Task 1: Weighted crosstabs
A single, reusable, documented R function that builds weighted proportion tables (survey outcome × demographic) with question wording attached — built with the survey package and designed so a teammate could run it on a different Verasight survey without editing its internals.


Task 2: Response density
How concentrated survey responses are among panelists — the smallest share of users who account for 50% of responses, overall and restricted to recent registrants, with interactive concentration (Lorenz) curves.


Task 3: Fraud detection
A design memo for a tiered workflow to flag potentially fraudulent open-ended responses: cheap deterministic signals → LLM classification on the flagged subset → human review, validated against labeled data and corroborated with closed-ended answers and paradata.


Task 4: Project scoping
Scoping questions and a time-intensity ranking across four hypothetical Verasight projects.

title: Verasight Data Scientist Case Study
text:
Approach & AI use
Code lives in code/ and utils/; write-ups in reports/. Generative AI was used throughout — the accompanying ai_memo.md documents the models, tools, harnessing, representative prompts, verification methods, and cases where the AI’s output was corrected.

title: Task 2 — Response Density
text: Task 2 — Response density. Measure how concentrated survey responses are among panelists. (1) Out of all users in the database, what is the smallest percent of users who account for 50% of all responses? (2) The same metric, restricted to users who registered in the last 90 days. (3) Visualize the full concentration curve — cumulative % of responses vs. cumulative % of users — with the 10%–90% thresholds marked.

title: Task 2 — Response Density
text:
Approach
“Density” here is a concentration question, so I treat it as a Lorenz curve: sort users from most to least active, accumulate their responses, and read off what share of users it takes to reach a given share of responses. The metric for parts 1–2 is the smallest share of users (most active first) whose responses reach 50% of the total. All logic lives in a small reusable helper, utils/density.R.

title: Task 2 — Response Density
text:
Data preparation
Before counting, two clean-ups materially change the answer:

Missing IDs. 23% of rows (43,692) have no user ID. These are unattributable to any panelist, so they are excluded — a naive count(ID) would otherwise lump them into one phantom “user” holding ~22% of all responses.
Duplicates. 3,863 exact-duplicate rows (same ID / survey / date) are de-duplicated.

Which users count as the denominator? “Users in the database” is ambiguous, and the choice changes the answer:

Responders — the 26,363 users who submitted at least one response. This is the universe I use.
All registered users — all 85,744 accounts, including 59,381 who have never responded.

Adding those tens of thousands of zero-response accounts makes responses look far more concentrated — the same 50% is then reached by only 4.8% of accounts, versus 15.7% among responders. I report the responder-based figure and state the choice explicitly so the number isn’t read out of context.

title: Task 2 — Response Density
text:
Results







Question
Answer




2.1 Smallest % of users for 50% of responses
15.7% (4,131 of 26,363), Gini 0.54


2.2 Same, registered in the last 90 days
28.3% (520 of 1,837), Gini 0.30



Task 2.2 reference date = the latest date in the data (2024-09-09); therefore, the 90-day cohort comprises registrations on or after 2024-06-11 through 2024-09-09.
Reading the result: responses are moderately concentrated — the most active 15.7% of responders produce half of all responses. New panelists are much less concentrated (28.3% needed for 50%): concentration builds with tenure as heavy responders accumulate history.

title: Task 2 — Response Density
text:
Concentration curves
Each curve plots cumulative % of responses (y) against cumulative % of users, most-active-first (x). The dashed diagonal is perfect equality; the further the navy curve bows above it, the more concentrated. Teal dots mark the 10%–90% thresholds; the red dot + guide lines mark the 50% answer. (Figures generated by code/02_task2_density.R.)

All responders



Figure 1: Response concentration across all responders (most active first). The dashed diagonal is perfect equality; the red point marks the 50% threshold (the base question).




Registered in the last 90 days



Figure 2: Response concentration among users registered in the last 90 days — visibly less concentrated than the full panel (the curve sits closer to the equality diagonal).

title: Task 2 — Response Density
text:
Assumptions & edge cases

File: brief said response-db.rds; actual file is data/full-response-db.rds.
Unattributable rows (missing ID) and exact duplicates are dropped (above).
Universe = responders; the all-registered alternative is reported for contrast.
“Last 90 days” is relative to the latest date in the data, not today.
Ties / zero-response users: users are ordered most-active-first and the cumulative curve is monotonic, so the smallest user count reaching a target is well defined; zero-response users appear only in the all-registered contrast.

title: Task 4 — Project Scoping & Prioritization
text:
Task 4 (preamble). “Included below are descriptions of 4 types of projects you could work on as a Data Scientist at Verasight. Read through the descriptions and then answer the questions below.”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4


Part 1: Scoping questions

“For each project, identify the 1-3 of the most important questions you would ask before beginning work. Your questions should help you clarify the project scope, the goals of the client, assess feasibility, and understand the specific data needs of the project. Questions may cover (but are not limited to) sampling frame creation, weighting or analysis requirements, deliverables, timelines, or client expectations. Assume you have the resources and training you need to complete individual tasks successfully — ask questions to help understand the scope and content of the projects, not on the mechanics of how to execute the tasks themselves.”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Part 1


Project 1: Nationally-representative survey (3,000 US adults)

“A nationally-representative survey of 3,000 US adults fielded from the Verasight panel. Questions were submitted by conference attendees. In addition to the standard deliverables of raw data, codebook, and survey weights, this project also requires a public-facing topline & crosstab report. Fielding & deliverable preparation should take 2 weeks.”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 1







My questions (1–3)




The questions submitted by conference attendees (I’m assuming I’d know the conference from my interactions with the client/sales teams (e.g., academic conference, a specific trade union, etc.)): The first thing I’d want to know is the type and format of questions that conference attendees have submitted. For example, do they have a coherent theme, how many are there, how many are free-response (which will be harder to analyze)? Are these very technical or are they meant for laity? Who has authority to edit them if necessary and how long might that take (if not me) to get them ready for fielding? And roughly how long will the final questionnaire run – I’d want a sense of the total length so it’s not too much of a burden on respondents and so it’s realistic to finalize and field in 2 weeks.
Cross-tab variables and required demographics: A public-facing topline & crosstab report requires a bit more preparation and understanding of the scrutiny they’ll face when made public. So I’d be concerned with knowing the most important demographics they require for fielding so I can reserve time to build out the topline & crosstab reports. Also, what are the cross-tab variables? Will they be all the key demographics or only a subset? What are the demographic requirements–e.g., employment status, ethnoracial distribution, political identification, educational attainment, etc.? I’d want to see if there were any hard to reach populations that might be hard to get in time or might create low Ns and therefore be unable to publish on a public-facing topline.
More details on the public-facing topline & crosstab report: A public-facing topline & crosstab report can come under varying levels of scrutiny depending on the intended audience, so I’d like to know who the client expects to read these findings/reports–e.g., news organizations, academic methodologists, the general public? This will also help me know the level of detail to include so it’s readable for the intended audience. If the questions are coming from conference participants, it could be commissioned by an organization that might have specific transparency and disclosure requirements, so I’d want to ask more about those. They might also have specific requirement criteria that might not have come up in the demographics conversation (e.g., diversity of individuals with specific disabilities, access to modes of communication, etc.)





Project 2: Wyoming voter survey (1,500 voters)

“A survey of 1,500 voters in Wyoming sampled from voter file records. The sales team notes that the client may have demographic quotas. Fielding should take 3 weeks.”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 2







My questions (1–3)




Follow up on quota samples: The note that “the client may have demographic quotas” is a bit vague, so I’d want more clarity on the quotas. Are they pretty standard and easy to fill or will they be more difficult and more granular? Since the sample is coming from a voting file, will there be partisan quotas–in Wyoming it might be much harder to find a 64-year-old college-educated Democrat than their Republican counterpart.
Voter file: I’d want to ask more about the voter file requirements? Who has the file, what is the source? Who will be doing the cleaning (even if they do the cleaning, I’d prob want to do my own quick quality assessment)? Also, how are we getting the sample? This framing implies the client is providing the dataset, so what will we merge it with to get a sample frame? And how do we actually reach these voters – a voter file gives us records, not a way to contact them, so do we have phones/addresses/emails, or are we matching to a panel?
What defines a voter: “Voter” can mean multiple things: voted in the last federal election, voted in one of the last 4 elections, simply registered, etc. Does the client have any stipulations around this?





Project 3: AI software-developer survey (300 developers)

“A survey of 300 software developers who program with AI models. The client would like to be able to weight the survey results to the population of eligible software developers. The timeline is 6 weeks to prepare and 4 weeks to field.”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 3







My questions (1–3)




Benchmarks and sample frame: What benchmarks exist that we could use? There’s no Census of software developers who build AI to define the population benchmarks. Perhaps there might be some Stack Overflow software developer survey or a niche BLS survey we could start from? Does the client have a population benchmark in mind already as a starting point? Is the “weight the survey results to the population of eligible software developers” negotiable if we have to weight to a soft target instead (e.g., using a Stack Overflow developer survey that might have problems instead of a more reliable survey like the US Census)? And once we settle on a benchmark, how are we actually sourcing the 300 developers – do we have enough of them on the Verasight panel or are we recruiting externally, and what’s the incidence of finding qualified AI devs?
Defining eligibility: How is the client defining eligibility here–full-time employed, working for specific companies or in specific industries? Are they developing a tool that uses AI or are they training an AI model? Are there regional, demographic, or skill requirements (e.g., background in CS, knows C++ vs Python, has a PhD, etc.)?
Content of the survey: The content of this survey seems not to have been developed – what will this be about? What kinds of questions will be asked? Who will prepare them? Will they be free response?





Project 4: Longitudinal survey of working women (1,000, 4 waves)

“A longitudinal survey of 1,000 working women. There are 4 waves, each a month apart. Fielding should start tomorrow.”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 4







My questions (1–3)




Retention plan? This is a longitudinal survey, meaning we’ll want to retain respondents over 4 waves. Retention will be the main concern for this project, so we’ll need clarity on the retention plan? How much will you pay/reimburse respondents? If that’s not enough, what wiggle room do we have to adjust it (i.e., what’s the budget)? What are the re-contact rules (how many times can we follow up with respondents)? Are there specific thresholds for retention? Is the 1,000 the wave 1 or wave 4 goal? What is time between waves? We may need to massively over-recruit to hit our goals.
“Fielding should start tomorrow.”? “Fielding should start tomorrow” implies the screener is already proofed, the questionnaire is already done, the sample frame is already pulled, etc. So, my first concern is what “ready” really means here? Are there any adjustments that need to be made, are there any potential issues that we might encounter since we didn’t prepare all of the materials? And where is the sample even coming from – is this the Verasight panel, or a list of working women the client is providing?
Working women? How is working women defined and what are the parameters? Are we looking for full-time or does part-time count? Is there a specific type of job or industry implied by “working” here? What do we do with respondents if their employment status changes during the study?






Part 2: Ranking by time intensity

“Now assume you receive answers to the questions you included above. Given what you know now, please rank these projects from 1-4 in order of how time intensive you think they will be for you as a Data Scientist. The project you rank first should be the project you expect to take the most of your time, and the project you rank 4th should be the project you expect to take the least amount of your time. Be explicit about the assumptions you make and provide reasoning for the ranking. (Note: There are no right or wrong answers for this question — we want to get insight into your thought process.)”
— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Part 2

My ranking (1 = most time-intensive, 4 = least):



Rank
Project




1
Project 3: AI software-developer survey


2
Project 4: Longitudinal survey of working women


3
Project 1: Nationally-representative survey


4
Project 2: Wyoming voter survey




1. Project 3: AI software-developer survey
I assume the responsibility to develop a sampling frame and benchmarks will be solely ours and constructing a weighting scheme for a population with no frame is open-ended methodological work that will require some time, skill, and creativity. It will require a comprehensive literature review to determine what potential data is out there, a complicated benchmark construction, lots of sensitivity checks, and a defensible writeup that will take time and probably require the eyes of other data scientists. While the timeframe is shorter than Project 4’s longitudinal survey, the detailed methodological work on this will require more effort.
If the client has a specific idea of their target sample and a good dataset in mind for benchmarking (and is OK with weighting to soft targets), then this could be an easier lift.


2. Project 4: Longitudinal survey of working women
Unless these are rigorous academics who have experience fielding their own surveys, I assume moderate editing to the materials before fielding can begin. I also assume “working women” will be somewhat loosely defined, not specific to any industry or occupation, which will make it easier for fielding. I assume that if someone becomes unemployed during the study, we’ll have to drop them from the sample, which will negatively impact retention rates. I also assume that by 1,000 person survey, they meant to start the first wave and that, with waves a month apart, the whole thing wraps in about 3-4 months.
So, the big lift for this project will be retention bc that’s not a huge sample for 4 waves where I’d expect a good amount of attrition. We’ll need to determine a large initial sample to ensure high enough retention rates and then ensure retention rates are of high enough quality as it proceeds and likely need to adjust incentives if the client has low incentives coming in. If the client has done quality preparation of the materials to the extent they do not need much editing and has a good incentive and retention strategy, that could lighten the expected work on this.


3. Project 1: Nationally-representative survey
Normally, I would assume projects using the internal Verasight data to be less demanding than others because of the familiarity with and tidiness of our sample; however, I think the squishiness of the survey questions and public-facing nature of this project make it more difficult than the remaining Wyoming voter survey. I assume most demanding part of this will be the unwieldy nature of the conference participants’ submitted questions. These will require organizing and rewriting to improve their precision and quality, many of them will be somewhat technical or jargon-y and will require translation, and I expect there to be a good handful of free response questions. Negotiating the editing of these questions with the client (who I assume will want to retain some level of control over the questions) and the back and forth will require the most effort for me.
I assume the topline & crosstab report and the scrutiny it will receive to be the second biggest time requirement here. I assume the client will come back with mostly generic demographic requirements for the crosstab but have one or two more difficult requirements (maybe a hard to reach demographic or one with low Ns). Since the client is affiliated with a conference, I’ll assume there’ll be some degree of the intended audience that will have some survey experience and want a more detailed than normal topline & crosstab report, so more time will have to be devoted here than in a normal one. If the client gives me full control over editing their questions and allows me to use my judgment to edit them (as long as I maintain the spirit of the question), that could shave off some of the work here.


4. Project 2: Wyoming voter survey
I assume this will come back as a standard state voter poll with fewer complications–standard demographic quotas and the client wants to define “voter” as anyone who voted in any of the last three elections. The bulk of the work will come from cleaning the voter file and building a sample frame, but the fielding will be straightforward enough – assuming the client hands us usable contact info (phones/addresses) or a panel match, since reaching voters off a bare file would make it noticeably harder. If I need to obtain and clean the voter file from scratch or if the demographic requirements are more complicated and hard to field, I could see this requiring more effort.
